questions.question: The nipple is defined as a circular smooth muscle structure typically overlying the:
questions.detail: The nipple overlies the 4th intercostal space and is composed of smooth muscle.

questions.question: How many ductal openings are typically found at the nipple?
questions.detail: There are typically 5 to 10 ductal openings present at the nipple surface.

questions.question: "Nipple Inversion" is defined as which of the following?
choices: The nipple being pulled back slightly
The nipple invaginating into the breast
questions.detail: Inversion specifically refers to the nipple invaginating into the breast tissue.

questions.question: When is nipple retraction or inversion considered most suspicious for underlying cancer?
choices: If it has been present since birth
If it is bilateral and chronic
If it occurs only during lactation
questions.detail: New changes in nipple position should raise concern for underlying cancers causing structural distortion.

questions.question: On a contrast-enhanced breast MRI, enhancement of the nipple is considered:
choices: An artifact of the breast coil
questions.detail: Nipple enhancement is a normal physiological finding on breast MRI and should not be confused with Paget’s disease.

questions.question: The axillary extension of fibroglandular tissue is known as the:
questions.detail: The Tail of Spence refers to the extension of breast tissue into the axilla.

questions.question: In which quadrant of the breast do most cancers typically originate?
questions.detail: The UOQ contains the most fibroglandular tissue, making it the most common site for cancer.

questions.question: Breast "Danger Zones" are areas where cancer often hides because there is typically:
questions.detail: The lack of dense tissue in these areas (medial, inferior, and retroglandular) allows cancers to hide.

questions.question: "Architectural distortion" on mammography is often caused by the straightening and tethering of:
questions.detail: Distortion of Cooper's ligaments is a primary cause of architectural distortion on imaging.

questions.question: For multiple-choice exams, an asymmetric "shrinking breast" should prompt suspicion for:
questions.detail: An asymmetric, shrinking breast is a classic buzzword for invasive lobular carcinoma.

questions.question: The functional "flower-shaped" milk-making unit of the breast is the:
questions.detail: Lobules are the actual milk-producing units of the breast tissue.

questions.question: The "Terminal Duct Lobular Unit" (TDLU) is clinically significant because it is:
choices: The only site of milk storage
The attachment point for Cooper's ligaments
questions.detail: The TDLU is the site of origin for the vast majority of breast cancers.

questions.question: Calcifications are particularly worrisome for cancer if they appear to follow the ductal system in which pattern?
questions.detail: Ductal patterns like linear or segmental calcifications are highly suspicious for malignancy.

questions.question: What is the name of the dilated portion of the major duct located under the nipple?
questions.detail: The lactiferous sinus is a normal dilated ductal segment found just below the nipple.

questions.question: What percentage of the breast's blood supply is provided by the internal mammary artery?
questions.detail: The internal mammary artery provides 60% of the blood supply to the breast.

questions.question: Approximately what percentage of breast lymph typically drains to the axilla?
questions.detail: The vast majority (97%) of breast lymphatic drainage goes to the axillary nodes.

questions.question: Axillary lymph node Level 2 is defined as being located:
choices: Medial and above the pectoralis minor
Between the pectoralis minor and major
questions.detail: Level 2 nodes are defined by their position deep to the pectoralis minor muscle.

questions.question: "Rotter Nodes" are found between which two muscles?
questions.detail: Interpectoral nodes, or Rotter nodes, are found between the major and minor pectoralis muscles.

questions.question: For staging purposes, Rotter nodes are treated at the same level as:
questions.detail: Rotter nodes are clinically staged at the same level as Level 2 nodes.

questions.question: Isolated metastases to internal mammary nodes are uncommon (3%) and are most often associated with cancer in which part of the breast?
questions.detail: Cancers in the medial breast are most likely to involve the internal mammary nodes.

questions.question: The Sternalis Muscle is a non-functional normal variant mass mimic seen in what percentage of people?
questions.detail: This non-functional muscle is present as a normal variant in 5% of the population.

questions.question: Which mammographic view is the ONLY one in which the Sternalis muscle is visible?
questions.detail: The sternalis muscle is visible only on the CC view and never on MLO.

questions.question: During which phase of the menstrual cycle are mammography and MRI best performed?
questions.detail: Imaging is best performed on days 7–14 when density and BPE are lower.

questions.question: Breast tenderness typically peaks at its maximum around which days of the menstrual cycle?
questions.detail: Maximum breast tenderness usually occurs at the very end of the cycle, days 27–30.

questions.question: Why is a biopsy of a prepubescent breast strongly discouraged?
choices: It can interfere with normal breast development
There are no terminal ducts yet
Lesions are always benign in this age group
questions.detail: Biopsy can damage the developing breast bud and interfere with maturation.

questions.question: Fibroadenomas characteristically degenerate and develop "popcorn" calcifications during which life stage?
questions.detail: Degeneration of fibroadenomas into popcorn calcifications is a hallmark of menopause.

questions.question: "Rod-like" secretory calcifications typically develop how many years after menopause?
questions.detail: Secretory rod-like calcifications typically appear 10–20 years after the onset of menopause.

questions.question: Which imaging modality has greater sensitivity in patients who are currently lactating?
questions.detail: Because lactating breasts are extremely dense, ultrasound is the more sensitive modality.

questions.question: What is the primary risk associated with biopsying a lactating breast?
questions.detail: A milk fistula is a major complication risk when biopsying the lactating breast.

questions.question: A "Galactocele" is a fat-containing benign lesion typically seen upon:
questions.detail: Galactoceles characteristically appear when a woman stops breastfeeding.

questions.question: How does a Galactocele characteristically appear on ultrasound or mammography?
choices: Aunt Minnie look with a fat-fluid level
questions.detail: A fat-fluid level on imaging is pathognomonic for a galactocele.

questions.question: "Lactating Adenomas" typically most closely resemble which other benign mass?
questions.detail: On imaging, lactating adenomas are virtually indistinguishable from fibroadenomas.

questions.question: What is the typical behavior of a lactating adenoma after breastfeeding stops?
choices: It causes a persistent milk fistula
It stays the same size permanently
questions.detail: These lesions typically regress quickly once lactation ends.

questions.question: Use of Hormone Replacement Therapy (specifically estrogen-progesterone combos) typically results in:
questions.detail: HRT use typically causes an increase in overall breast density.

questions.question: Nipple "Retraction" is distinguished from "Inversion" because the nipple is:
questions.detail: Retraction is a milder pulling back, whereas inversion is a full invagination.

questions.question: A standard screening mammogram in the United States consists of which two views?
questions.detail: The standard two-view screening set includes the CC and MLO views.

questions.question: For an MLO view to be technically adequate, the nipple must be:
choices: In profile in at least one view
It does not need to be in profile
questions.detail: Adequate positioning requires the nipple to be in profile in at least one view.

questions.question: On a technically adequate MLO view, the pectoralis muscle must be visualized down to the level of the:
questions.detail: The pectoral muscle should reach the PNL or further down to ensure adequate coverage.

questions.question: When mirror-imaging both MLO views, the pectoralis muscles should ideally form which shape?
questions.detail: The pectorals should form a symmetric V or U shape when compared side-by-side.

questions.question: Nipple pointing on a CC view is considered "unacceptable" if the nipple is pointing:
questions.detail: Sideways pointing on a CC view is a positioning error and is considered unacceptable.

questions.question: The Posterior Nipple Line (PNL) measurement on the CC view must be within how many cm of the MLO measurement?
questions.detail: The PNL measurements between views must agree within 1 cm for technical adequacy.

questions.question: "Camel Nose" is a buzzword describing a breast on the MLO view that:
choices: Has NOT been pulled "up and out" and sags low
questions.detail: Camel nose describes a sagging breast that was not properly lifted by the technician.

questions.question: Ideally, which anatomical landmark should be visualized at the bottom of the MLO view?
questions.detail: Visualizing the inframammary fold is a key requirement for a good MLO view.

questions.question: For mammography positioning, relaxed pectoralis muscles are preferred and should appear:
questions.detail: A convex border indicates the muscle is relaxed, allowing more tissue to be shown.

questions.question: When should an LMO (Lateral-Medial Oblique) view be used instead of a standard MLO?
choices: For women with kyphosis or to avoid a pacemaker
questions.detail: LMO is the preferred oblique view for patients with kyphosis or medial chest devices.

questions.question: Which mammographic view is known to contain the MOST breast tissue of all possible views?
questions.detail: The MLO view provides the most extensive anatomical coverage of the breast.

questions.question: When performing spot compression views, the clinical recommendation is to:
questions.detail: Leaving the collimator open provides more surrounding landmarks for orientation.

questions.question: Magnification views are primarily obtained to help characterize:
questions.detail: Mag views are the primary tool for detailed evaluation of calcifications.

questions.question: If a lesion is seen on the MLO only (and not on the CC), which 90-degree view should you pick next?
questions.detail: Because most cancers are lateral, the ML view is the logical next step.

questions.question: Regulating bodies (MQSA) demand a Positive Predictive Value (PPV1) for screeners of approximately:
questions.detail: A PPV1 of around 4% is the target standard for screening mammography.

questions.question: How many cancers per 1000 mammograms are radiologists typically trying to find?
questions.detail: The goal for a quality screening program is to detect 3–8 cancers per 1000 patients.

questions.question: To make early changes easier to see, it is recommended to compare current mammograms to films from how many years prior?
questions.detail: Comparing to a study from 2 years ago is best for spotting subtle changes over time.

questions.question: Motion artifact in mammography typically predominates in which part of the breast?
questions.detail: The inferior breast is most prone to motion blur due to less effective compression.

questions.question: If you lack adequate coverage of the posterior lateral edge on a standard CC view, the next appropriate step is:
questions.detail: XCCL is specifically used to image lateral tissue missed on the standard CC.

questions.question: The ML (Mediolateral) view is a 90-degree view that shows which part of the breast in better detail?
questions.detail: ML provides better resolution for lateral tissue as it is closer to the detector.

questions.question: The LM (Lateromedial) view shows which part of the breast in better detail?
questions.detail: LM provides better resolution for medial tissue by placing it closer to the detector.

questions.question: If a "nodule" is seen ONLY on the CC view, which special view should be performed?
questions.detail: Rolled CC views help confirm and localize findings seen only on the CC view.

questions.question: Which mammographic view is used if a "nodule" is suspected to be located in the skin?
questions.detail: The tangential view is used to definitively place a finding within the skin.

questions.question: Which view is the "best in life" for identifying "milk of calcium" and its characteristic layering?
questions.detail: A 90-degree true lateral is essential to see the tea-cupping of milk of calcium.

questions.question: Which view is best for a "nodule" located in the far posterior medial breast (the "cleavage")?
questions.detail: The cleavage view (double breast CC) targets far posterior medial tissue.

questions.question: What is the standard set of views for a patient with breast implants?
questions.detail: Eklund views displace the implant to allow visualization of surrounding tissue.

questions.question: True or False: Grids are typically used during magnification views to improve resolution.
questions.detail: Grids are NOT used in magnification views to prevent excessive radiation dose.

questions.question: Blur on a mammogram can be caused by patient motion, exposure being too long, or:
questions.detail: Inadequate exposure time (either too long or too short) can cause image blur.

questions.question: "Horizontal lines" on a mammogram are an artifact likely representing:
questions.detail: Horizontal line artifacts are typically caused by grid malfunctions.

questions.question: To reduce motion artifact inferiorly, techs use which specific maneuver?
questions.detail: The 'sweep up and out' maneuver ensures better compression and less inferior blur.

questions.question: On an adequate MLO view, the pectoral muscle should be relaxed, creating a border that is:
questions.detail: A convex pectoral border is a sign of a relaxed muscle and proper positioning.

questions.question: In an adequate CC view, the chest wall to nipple distance should be within how many cm of the distance on the MLO?
questions.detail: The distances must be within 1 cm of each other for the CC view to be adequate.

questions.question: Triangulation strategy: Lesions that are MEDIAL to the nipple will do what on a true lateral view?
questions.detail: Medial lesions appear higher (rise) on a true lateral compared to the MLO.

questions.question: Triangulation strategy: Lesions that are LATERAL to the nipple will do what on a true lateral view?
questions.detail: Lateral lesions appear lower (sink) on a true lateral compared to the MLO.

questions.question: What is the main reason localization "voodoo" (triangulation) is necessary?
choices: Cancers only occur in the UOQ
LOQ nodules can project above the nipple on MLO
All lesions are seen on CC
questions.detail: Triangulation is needed because LOQ nodules project higher than the nipple on MLO.

questions.question: True or False: If a patient under 30 has a palpable lump, the first step is typically to skip the mammogram and go straight to ultrasound.
questions.detail: For patients under 30, ultrasound is the standard initial imaging modality.

questions.question: The Posterior Nipple Line (PNL) on a CC view is measured from the nipple back to the:
questions.detail: The PNL measurement on the CC view is taken from the nipple to the chest wall.

questions.question: In mirror-imaged MLOs, pectorals forming a "V" or "U" indicates:
questions.detail: This symmetric V or U appearance is a indicator of proper technologist positioning.

questions.question: The CC view is designed to maximize the visualization of which tissue?
questions.detail: The CC view is particularly effective at imaging posterior medial breast tissue.

questions.question: "Focal skin thickening" is most worrisome for cancer when it is associated with:
questions.detail: Associated masses or nipple changes make skin thickening much more suspicious.

questions.question: A lesion seen in the MLO view only that is MEDIAL on the CC film will do what on the true lateral (ML)?
questions.detail: Medial lesions rise on the lateral view (Muffins Rise).

questions.question: A lesion seen in the MLO only that is LATERAL on the CC film will do what on the true lateral (ML)?
questions.detail: Lateral lesions sink on the lateral view (Lead Sinks).

questions.question: The popular mnemonic for localizing lesions seen on MLO only is:
questions.detail: This is the classic mnemonic for breast lesion triangulation.

questions.question: Which area of the breast is considered "high risk" for being excluded from the MLO view?
questions.detail: The medial breast tissue is the most commonly missed area on an MLO view.

questions.question: Which area of the breast is "high risk" for being excluded from the CC view?
questions.detail: Inferior posterior tissue is the area most often excluded from the CC view.

questions.question: In the clock face localization tool, the PNL for both breasts starts in the upper inner quadrant and exits through the:
questions.detail: The PNL extends from UIQ through the nipple to the outer quadrant.

questions.question: "Nodules" located ABOVE the level of the nipple will project where on the MLO view?
choices: They do not project at all
questions.detail: Nodules superior to the nipple project to a lower position on the MLO view.

questions.question: "Nodules" located BELOW the level of the nipple will project where on the MLO view?
choices: ABOVE the level of the nipple
questions.detail: Nodules inferior to the nipple project to a higher position on the MLO view.

questions.question: If a tech rolls the breast medially and a superior tumor moves medial, this confirms the tumor is:
questions.detail: Superior tumors move in the same direction as the roll (medial roll = medial move).

questions.question: If a tech rolls the breast medially and a tumor moves lateral, the tumor is located in the:
questions.detail: Inferior tumors move in the opposite direction of the roll.

questions.question: If you roll the breast LATERALLY and a superior tumor moves lateral, this confirms it is:
questions.detail: A superior tumor will follow the direction of any roll (lateral roll = lateral move).

questions.question: Localization Step 1 involves finding the "nodule" on the MLO and determining if it is:
questions.detail: The first step is localizing the lesion relative to the PNL on the MLO view.

questions.question: In "Practice Case 1," a nodule below the PNL on MLO that is medial on CC will:
choices: Rise on the lateral (to a position above the nipple)
questions.detail: Because it is medial, it must rise on the lateral view.

questions.question: Superior vs. Inferior location of a breast lesion is definitively inferred based on:
choices: Distance from the nipple on CC
How the lesion moves when the tech rolls the breast
questions.detail: Roll views are the definitive way to distinguish superior from inferior location.

questions.question: On the CC view, the primary goal for localization is to determine if a nodule is:
questions.detail: The CC view's main role in localization is determining medial vs. lateral position.

questions.question: If a nodule is 7mm above the PNL on MLO and is lateral on CC, it will:
questions.detail: Lateral lesions sink on the lateral view (Lead Sinks).

questions.question: A "medial" lesion seen on MLO only will do what on the true lateral (ML)?
questions.detail: Medial lesions rise relative to their MLO position (Muffins Rise).

questions.question: A "lateral" lesion seen on MLO only will do what on the true lateral (ML)?
questions.detail: Lateral lesions sink relative to their MLO position (Lead Sinks).

questions.question: In a medial roll view, an inferior tumor moves:
questions.detail: Inferior tumors move opposite to the roll; a medial roll causes a lateral move.

questions.question: In a lateral roll view, an inferior tumor moves:
questions.detail: Because inferior moves opposite, a lateral roll results in medial movement.

questions.question: If a lesion is seen ONLY in the CC view, it is localized using which technique?
questions.detail: The Rolled CC is the standard maneuver for a lesion seen only on CC.

questions.question: The "central problem" of localization is that LOQ nodules project above the nipple on MLO and UIQ nodules project:
questions.detail: This counter-intuitive projection (UIQ projects below) is why triangulation is critical.
